quick_facts.label: Role
quick_facts.value: A Lieutenant in the Judge Advocate General (JAG) Branch is a commissioned Indian Army legal officer. The JAG Department is the Army’s legal branch and handles military disciplinary cases and litigation.

quick_facts.label: Entry status
quick_facts.value: The JAG Entry Scheme grants a Short Service Commission (Non-Technical) in the Indian Army’s JAG Branch to eligible law graduates; the specific conditions are set separately in each course notification.

quick_facts.label: Current recruitment context
quick_facts.value: For the 125th JAG Course, commencing April 2027, the July 2026 notification context reported 10 combined vacancies for men and women and an online application window from July 17, 2026 to August 17, 2026. Vacancies, dates, and eligibility must be checked against the live official notification before applying.

quick_facts.label: Training
quick_facts.value: The preceding JAG Entry notification for the October 2026 course specified 49 weeks of pre-commission training at Officers Training Academy (OTA), Chennai. Training terms can change by notification.

quick_facts.label: Commission terms
quick_facts.value: The October 2026 JAG notification described an initial 10-year Short Service Commission, extendable by four years, with eligible and suitable officers considered for Permanent Commission in the 10th year under policies in force at that time.

sections.heading: Legal and military-justice responsibilities
paragraphs: The Judge Advocate General’s Department is the Indian Army’s legal branch. The Ministry of Defence has stated that it deals with military-related disciplinary cases and litigation; the Judge Advocate General advises the Chief of Army Staff on military, martial, and international law, and assists the Adjutant General on discipline involving military law.

As a newly commissioned Lieutenant in this branch, the role is therefore an officer-and-lawyer post within that legal function: supporting the application of military law and discipline while operating within the Army’s commissioned-officer structure. The Army Rules describe a judge advocate’s court-martial function as representing the Judge-Advocate-General, identifying defects or irregularities in proceedings, advising on legal questions, and maintaining impartiality.
bullets: Support legal work concerning military discipline, disciplinary cases, and litigation.
Provide or contribute to legal advice within the Army’s military-law framework.
Where appointed to court-martial duties, identify procedural irregularities, advise on legal matters before the court, and act impartially.

sections.heading: Route through the JAG Entry Scheme
paragraphs: This role is reached through the Indian Army’s JAG Entry Scheme, rather than through a general law-firm recruitment route. The July 2026 notification for the 125th course described a Short Service Commission JAG course for unmarried male and female law graduates, due to commence in April 2027; applicants should rely on the official Join Indian Army notification because course numbers, dates, vacancy counts, and selection rules are periodically revised.

The immediately preceding official-course text for the 124th course required a three-year LL.B. after graduation or five-year LL.B. with at least 55% aggregate marks, eligibility for Bar Council registration, and the specified CLAT PG score. It described selection as application shortlisting, a two-stage five-day SSB interview, medical examination after SSB recommendation, and final joining in merit order subject to vacancies and eligibility.

That notification also specified 49 weeks of pre-commission training at OTA Chennai. Successful completion of training is the pathway to commission; applicants should not treat eligibility or selection as an assured appointment.
bullets: Monitor the Join Indian Army officer-entry portal and read the course-specific PDF in full.
Hold the law qualification, Bar Council eligibility, age, nationality, marital-status, and CLAT PG credentials specified in the applicable notification.
Complete the Army’s shortlisting, SSB, medical, document-verification, merit, and OTA-training requirements.

sections.heading: Work setting
paragraphs: The workplace is the Indian Army’s legal and disciplinary environment rather than a civilian legal practice. The JAG Department’s stated remit includes military disciplinary cases and litigation, while the statutory court-martial role of a judge advocate includes advising the court on law and procedure. Assignments, locations, and the exact balance of advisory, disciplinary, and litigation work are determined by Army requirements rather than guaranteed by the entry scheme.
bullets: Work is carried out as a commissioned Army officer in the JAG Department.
The role may involve military-law advice, disciplinary matters, litigation, and court-martial-related legal duties.
Service conditions and postings are governed by Army requirements and applicable rules.

sections.heading: Service progression and commission context
paragraphs: The JAG route is advertised as a Short Service Commission. In the October 2026 course notification, the initial commission was stated as 10 years, extendable by four years; eligible and suitable officers could be considered for Permanent Commission in their 10th year under prevailing policy. This is policy-dependent, not an automatic conversion to Permanent Commission.

The same notification notes that reckonable commissioned service runs from the date of grant of commission and discusses seniority adjustment for Short Service Commission officers at the first substantive rank of Captain. Actual promotion, seniority, retention, extension, and Permanent Commission outcomes depend on the rules and policies applicable to the officer and course at the time.
bullets: Begin as a Short Service Commission officer in the JAG Branch.
A Permanent Commission may be considered under prevailing policy; it is not guaranteed by selection through JAG entry.
Treat promotion and tenure rules as variable service-policy matters and verify the current notification and Army orders.

sections.heading: Compensation context
paragraphs: Compensation is set by government pay and allowance rules, rank, and posting conditions—not by a private-sector salary offer. The October 2026 JAG notification stated that, upon successful commissioning, pay would be fixed at the first cell of Pay Level 10; it also listed Military Service Pay of ₹15,500 per month for ranks from Lieutenant through Brigadier.

The same notification stated that field-area allowances depend on rank and area of posting, and expressly noted that pay, allowances, and associated rules are subject to revision. Accordingly, these figures are dated notification context, not a guaranteed current take-home amount.
bullets: Dated 2026 notification context: commissioning pay was to begin at the first cell of Pay Level 10.
Dated 2026 notification context: Military Service Pay was listed as ₹15,500 per month from Lieutenant to Brigadier.
Allowances and net pay vary with posting, entitlement, deductions, and later government revisions.
